message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate test: the next it.todo (sword (cap 2000), second successive claim 1500 → payout 600 G (capped), remainingCap 0 G)
Current state: 36 tests passing, 7 todo
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

This test requires the claim() function to support cap PERSISTENCE across successive claims and payout CAPPING. The first claim already left remainingCap 600. The second claim of 1500 would compute reimbursable 1400, but it is CAPPED to the remaining cap of 600 → payout 600, remainingCap 0.

Design: extend claim() to accept the current remaining cap as an optional parameter (e.g. claim(items, incident, currentCap?) where currentCap defaults to the full cap = 2×insurance sum). The payout is min(computed payout, currentCap); remainingCap = currentCap - payout. So the second claim test would call claim(items, incident, 600) and expect { payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }. Choose the cleanest signature consistent with the existing claim() contract (the CLI will thread remaining cap between successive claims on the same policy).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Green after completion. You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim (both Compilation Prediction and Runtime Prediction lines, each with Correct or Incorrect). Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two prediction lines into one.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Find cap persistence rationale in spec

content.content:

111:- two swords → insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G.

120:- sword (sum 1000, cap 2000), two successive claims of 1500 each:
121:  - first claim → payout 1400 G (1500 − 100), cap remaining 600 G.
122:  - second claim → payout 600 G (desired 1400 capped to remaining), cap remaining 0 G.

167:- claim result: `{payout: <integer>, remainingCap: <integer>}`.
225:30. cap = 2× insurance sum (sword+amulet 1600 → cap 3200) — R5 cap value.
226:31. cap based on unmodified value (cursed sword → cap 2000) — R5 cap.

content.text:
The spec confirms: the second successive claim of 1500 computes a desired payout of 1400, but it is capped to the remaining cap of 600, leaving remainingCap 0.

Per the design guidance, I'll thread the current remaining cap as an optional third parameter `claim(items, incident, currentCap?)`. The test will call `claim(items, incident, 600)` and expect `{ payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }`.

Let me check the test file template to see whether the todo already specifies the call shape, or whether I write it.

The current `it.todo` only has a label. I'll activate it as executable code. Now let me check the current behavior: `claim()` accepts only `(items, incident)`. Calling it with a third argument `600` in TypeScript would be a compilation error (too many arguments) under strict typing — but the journal shows esbuild strips types and is lenient, so it won't fail to compile. With the current 2-arg signature, the third argument is ignored; the function would compute payout 1400, remainingCap = cap(2000) − 1400 = 600. So it returns `{payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600}` vs expected `{payout: 600, remainingCap: 0}`.

## Step 1: Activate One Test

content.text:
As predicted (per the established toolchain behavior), there was NO compilation error — esbuild stripped the types and ignored the third argument. The test ran straight to the assertion failure.

The function ignored the third argument `600` and computed payout = max(0, 1500−100) = 1400, remainingCap = cap(2000) − 1400 = 600. This is a runtime assertion error.

Since there was no compilation error, there is no separate "create empty function" stage needed — the function already exists. We are already at the runtime/assertion error stage, which is exactly what I predicted.

## Step 5: Make Prediction - Runtime Error

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "sword (cap 2000), second successive claim 1500 → payout 600 G (capped), remainingCap 0 G"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: { payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }
- Actual value: { payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600 } (third arg ignored; payout uncapped 1400, remainingCap = 2000 − 1400)
- Diff:
  Expected: { payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }
  Received: { payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600 }
```

## Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Already verified above — the test fails with exactly the assertion error predicted: `expected { payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600 } to deeply equal { payout: 600, remainingCap: +0 }`.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "sword (cap 2000), second successive claim 1500 → payout 600 G (capped), remainingCap 0 G"
**Compilation Prediction**: "Expected 2 arguments, but got 3" ❌ Incorrect — esbuild strips types and does not type-check (consistent with cycles 24-35), so no compilation error occurred; ran straight to the assertion as I noted in the prediction.
**Runtime Prediction**: expected { payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600 } to deeply equal { payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 } ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error — the third argument (currentCap=600) is ignored, payout is uncapped at 1400, remainingCap = 2000 − 1400 = 600.

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

content.text:
Red phase complete for the cap-persistence test.

Summary:
- Activated test: "sword (cap 2000), second successive claim 1500 → payout 600 G (capped), remainingCap 0 G" in `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts` (line ~290).
- The test calls `claim(items, incident, 600)` — threading the remaining cap as a third argument — and expects `{ payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }`.
- Compilation prediction was Incorrect (esbuild strips types, no "too many arguments" error — consistent with prior cycles); Runtime prediction was Correct: `{ payout: 1400, remainingCap: 600 }` vs expected `{ payout: 600, remainingCap: 0 }`.
- Implementation file `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` currently has `claim(items, incident)` with no `currentCap` parameter (line 138) and no payout-capping logic.

For the Green phase: extend `claim()` to accept an optional `currentCap` (defaulting to `capFor(items)`), cap the payout to `min(payout, currentCap)`, and compute `remainingCap = currentCap - payout`. Current state: 37 tests passing once green, 6 todo (all CLI/error-case tests).

State: 36 passing, 1 failing (the just-activated test), 6 todo. Proceeding to Green phase.
